title: Motherboards, CPUs & Firmware (ATX, Sockets, Buses, BIOS/UEFI, TPM)
summary: Covers motherboard form factors and chipsets, expansion buses and PCIe lanes, CPU architectures/sockets/cores/cache/TDP, and firmware including BIOS vs UEFI, POST, CMOS, Secure Boot and TPM.
content:
The motherboard is the central printed circuit board that interconnects every component via traces, buses, sockets, and connectors. Form factor dictates physical size, mounting-hole pattern, and slot count, and must match the case and power supply. The three exam-critical sizes are ATX (305 x 244 mm, up to 7 expansion slots, multiple RAM slots and PCIe x16 slots), microATX/mATX (244 x 244 mm, up to 4 slots — the common embassy desktop), and Mini-ITX (170 x 170 mm, one expansion slot, two RAM slots — used for compact secure terminals, kiosks, and thin clients). All three share the same back-panel I/O and mounting standard, so a smaller board fits a larger case but not vice versa.

The chipset is the silicon that brokers communication between the CPU, memory, expansion slots, and peripherals. Historically this was split into a Northbridge (high-speed: memory and graphics) and a Southbridge (I/O: SATA, USB, audio, legacy). Modern designs have absorbed the memory controller and PCIe graphics lanes directly into the CPU die, leaving a single Platform Controller Hub (PCH) for slower I/O, linked to the CPU by a dedicated bus (Intel DMI / AMD Infinity Fabric). The chipset determines which CPUs, RAM speeds, and how many PCIe lanes/USB ports a board supports.

A bus is a shared electrical pathway. The dominant expansion bus is PCI Express (PCIe), a point-to-point serial interface measured in lanes. Slot width is denoted x1, x4, x8, or x16 (physical/electrical lane count); a x16 slot is used for GPUs, x1 for NICs/sound/capture cards. Generations roughly double per-lane bandwidth: PCIe 3.0 ~1 GB/s per lane, PCIe 4.0 ~2 GB/s, PCIe 5.0 ~4 GB/s (so a PCIe 4.0 x16 link is ~32 GB/s). PCIe is backward/forward compatible — a card negotiates down to the lowest common generation and lane count. A longer slot can accept a shorter card (x4 card in an x16 slot), and many slots are physically x16 but electrically wired to fewer lanes.

The CPU (processor) executes instructions. The two architectures to know: x86-64 (also AMD64/Intel 64) is the 64-bit extension of the x86 instruction set used by Intel/AMD desktops and servers — CISC, high power and performance. ARM is a RISC architecture dominating phones, tablets, Apple Silicon Macs, and Chromebooks — far better performance-per-watt, which is why it appears in low-power and battery devices. 32-bit (x86) CPUs/OSes can address only ~4 GB of RAM; 64-bit addresses vastly more (terabytes) and is required for modern memory and security features. A 64-bit CPU can run a 32-bit OS, but a 32-bit CPU cannot run a 64-bit OS.

The CPU attaches to a socket. Intel mainstream uses LGA (Land Grid Array) — the pins are on the socket/motherboard and the CPU has flat contact pads (e.g., LGA1700, LGA1200). AMD historically used PGA (Pin Grid Array) — pins on the CPU, holes in the socket (e.g., AM4); AMD's AM5 moved to LGA. The practical exam point: with LGA, bent pins damage the (expensive) board; with PGA, bent pins are on the CPU. The socket and chipset together define CPU compatibility — never assume a CPU drops into any board of the same brand.

Performance characteristics: a core is an independent execution unit; multi-core CPUs (dual/quad/hexa/octa) run threads in true parallel. Simultaneous Multithreading — Intel's brand is Hyper-Threading — presents each physical core as two logical processors, so an 8-core HT CPU shows 16 threads, improving throughput on parallel workloads (not doubling performance). Clock speed (GHz) is cycles per second; Turbo Boost / Precision Boost temporarily raises clocks above the base frequency within thermal/power limits. TDP (Thermal Design Power, in watts) is the heat the cooler must dissipate at base load — it sizes the cooling solution, not exact power draw.

Cache is small, very fast SRAM on the CPU die that hides slow main-memory latency, organized in levels: L1 is smallest and fastest (per-core, split instruction/data, tens of KB); L2 is larger and slower (per-core, hundreds of KB to a few MB); L3 is largest and slowest of the three (shared across all cores, tens of MB). The hierarchy is checked L1 to L2 to L3 to RAM; a hit higher up saves enormous time. Many CPUs include integrated graphics (an iGPU on the CPU die, e.g., Intel UHD/Iris, AMD Radeon/APU) that share system RAM — adequate for office/diplomatic productivity work but weak for 3D/GPU compute, where a discrete card in the PCIe x16 slot is needed.

The CPU is cooled through a thermal interface. A heat-conductive thermal paste (compound) fills microscopic air gaps between the CPU integrated heat spreader (IHS) and the cooler base; air gaps insulate, so a thin even layer is essential. Cooling options: a passive heatsink (fins, no fan) for low-TDP/silent designs; the standard active heatsink-and-fan (HSF); or liquid/AIO cooling (pump, radiator, fans) for high-TDP chips. Reseating or replacing dried-out paste is a classic fix for a CPU that overheats and throttles or shuts down.

Firmware is the low-level code stored on a motherboard chip that initializes hardware before the OS loads. BIOS (Basic Input/Output System) is the legacy 16-bit firmware: text menus, boots from MBR disks, and is limited to drives up to ~2.2 TB. UEFI (Unified Extensible Firmware Interface) is the modern replacement: a graphical, mouse-driven interface with networking and pre-boot apps, support for GPT disks beyond 2.2 TB, faster boot, and the Secure Boot feature. On power-up, firmware runs POST (Power-On Self-Test) to verify CPU, RAM, and essential hardware; failures are signaled by beep codes or POST/diagnostic LEDs before any video is available.

Firmware settings and the real-time clock are kept powered by the CMOS battery — a coin cell (typically CR2032) on the board. A dead CMOS battery causes the system clock to reset (wrong date/time) and BIOS/UEFI settings (including boot order) to revert to defaults each power-off; replacing the CR2032 is the standard fix. Boot order (boot sequence) in firmware sets which device the system tries first (internal SSD, USB, network/PXE) — set incorrectly, it boots the wrong media or reports 'no operating system found.' Resetting firmware to defaults is done via a menu option, a CLR_CMOS jumper, or removing the battery.

Security-critical firmware features for an embassy environment: Secure Boot is a UEFI feature that verifies the digital signature of the bootloader/OS against trusted keys, blocking unsigned rootkits and boot-level malware. TPM (Trusted Platform Module) is a dedicated cryptoprocessor (discrete chip or firmware fTPM/PTT in the CPU) that securely stores encryption keys, passwords, and platform-integrity measurements; it is what backs BitLocker full-disk encryption and is required for Windows 11 (TPM 2.0). Together, Secure Boot + TPM establish a hardware root of trust — exactly what is needed to protect classified data at rest on diplomatic endpoints.

Firmware updates (flashing the BIOS/UEFI) add CPU support, fix bugs, and patch security vulnerabilities. They must be done carefully: use the exact model's firmware, ensure stable power (a UPS), and never interrupt the process — a failed flash can brick the board (some boards offer BIOS flashback / dual-BIOS recovery). In a managed environment, firmware updates are part of the patch lifecycle alongside OS and driver updates, since firmware-level vulnerabilities sit below the operating system's defenses.
keyPoints: Form factors: ATX (305x244mm, up to 7 slots) > microATX (244x244, up to 4) > Mini-ITX (170x170, 1 slot); smaller fits a bigger case, not vice versa
Modern CPUs absorb the memory controller and GPU PCIe lanes; the chipset/PCH handles slower I/O over DMI/Infinity Fabric
PCIe is serial/point-to-point by lanes (x1/x4/x8/x16); each gen ~doubles per-lane bandwidth (3.0~1, 4.0~2, 5.0~4 GB/s per lane); a shorter card works in a longer slot
x86-64 = CISC, high performance (Intel/AMD); ARM = RISC, best performance-per-watt (phones, Apple Silicon, Chromebooks)
32-bit addresses ~4GB; 64-bit addresses terabytes; a 64-bit CPU runs a 32-bit OS but not vice versa
LGA = pins on the board (Intel, AMD AM5); PGA = pins on the CPU (AMD AM4); socket + chipset define CPU compatibility
Cores = true parallelism; Hyper-Threading/SMT = 2 logical threads per core; Turbo raises clocks within limits; TDP = heat the cooler must handle
Cache speed/size: L1 fastest/smallest (per-core) > L2 (per-core) > L3 largest/slowest (shared); checked L1>L2>L3>RAM
Thermal paste fills air gaps between CPU IHS and cooler; dried paste causes overheating/throttling
BIOS = legacy, MBR, ~2.2TB limit; UEFI = GUI, GPT >2.2TB, faster, supports Secure Boot
POST checks core hardware (beep codes/LEDs); dead CMOS (CR2032) battery resets clock and BIOS settings/boot order
Secure Boot verifies signed bootloaders; TPM 2.0 stores keys (backs BitLocker, required for Windows 11) = hardware root of trust

flashcards.front: Three motherboard form factors smallest to largest
flashcards.back: Mini-ITX (170x170mm, 1 slot) < microATX (244x244, up to 4) < ATX (305x244, up to 7)

flashcards.front: What does the chipset / PCH do today?
flashcards.back: Handles slower I/O (SATA, USB, audio) and links to the CPU via DMI/Infinity Fabric; the memory controller and GPU PCIe lanes now live on the CPU die

flashcards.front: What does a PCIe x16 vs x1 slot indicate?
flashcards.back: Lane count: x16 (16 lanes) for GPUs; x1 for NICs, sound, and capture cards
~1 / ~2 / ~4 GB/s per lane (roughly doubles each generation)
x86-64 = CISC, high performance (Intel/AMD); ARM = RISC, best performance-per-watt (phones, Apple Silicon, Chromebooks)

flashcards.front: Can a 32-bit CPU run a 64-bit OS?
flashcards.back: No. A 64-bit CPU can run a 32-bit OS, but not the reverse; 32-bit caps usable RAM at ~4 GB
LGA = pins on the motherboard, pads on CPU (Intel, AMD AM5); PGA = pins on the CPU (AMD AM4)
Presents each physical core as 2 logical threads to improve parallel throughput (not double performance)
Thermal Design Power (watts) — the heat the cooler must dissipate at base load; sizes the cooling solution
L1 fastest/smallest (per-core) > L2 (per-core) > L3 largest/slowest (shared across cores)
It fills microscopic air gaps between the CPU IHS and cooler base; air insulates, so a thin even layer maximizes heat transfer
BIOS = legacy, MBR-only, ~2.2TB drive limit; UEFI = GUI, GPT support beyond 2.2TB, faster boot, Secure Boot
Power-On Self-Test — firmware checks CPU/RAM/core hardware at startup; errors reported via beep codes or diagnostic LEDs

flashcards.front: Symptoms of a dead CMOS battery
flashcards.back: Clock resets to wrong date/time and BIOS settings (incl. boot order) revert to defaults each power-off; replace the CR2032
UEFI feature that verifies the bootloader/OS digital signature against trusted keys to block boot-level malware/rootkits

flashcards.front: What is a TPM and what does it back?
flashcards.back: Trusted Platform Module — cryptoprocessor storing keys/passwords/integrity measurements; backs BitLocker and is required (2.0) for Windows 11

flashcards.front: Risk of a BIOS/UEFI flash and how to mitigate
flashcards.back: Interruption/wrong file can brick the board; use exact model firmware, stable power (UPS), and BIOS flashback/dual-BIOS if available
A GPU on the CPU die sharing system RAM; fine for productivity, weak for 3D/GPU compute (use a discrete card in the x16 slot)

title: Memory & Storage (DDR3/4/5, ECC, Channels, HDD/SSD/NVMe, RAID, MBR/GPT)
summary: Covers RAM generations/pins/voltages, DIMM vs SO-DIMM, ECC and channel architectures, HDD vs SSD/NVMe storage and interfaces, RAID levels, and MBR vs GPT partitioning with common file systems.
content:
RAM (Random Access Memory) is volatile working memory — fast but cleared on power loss. Modern systems use DDR SDRAM (Double Data Rate), which transfers data on both the rising and falling edge of the clock. The generation matters because each is keyed differently and is NOT interchangeable: DDR3 (240-pin DIMM, 1.5V, typically 800-2133 MT/s) ; DDR4 (288-pin DIMM, 1.2V, ~2133-3200+ MT/s, denser modules up to 32 GB+); DDR5 (288-pin DIMM but a different notch, 1.1V, starting ~4800 MT/s, on-module power management and higher capacity). Lower voltage each generation improves efficiency; the physical keying/notch position prevents inserting the wrong type into a slot.

Form factor distinguishes the module. A DIMM (Dual Inline Memory Module) is the full-size stick for desktops and servers. A SO-DIMM (Small Outline DIMM) is the shorter module for laptops, mini-PCs, and many all-in-ones (DDR4 SO-DIMM = 260 pins, DDR3 SO-DIMM = 204 pins). They are not cross-compatible with desktop slots. RAM speed is rated in MT/s (mega-transfers per second, often loosely called MHz); the module's data rate must be supported by the CPU/board, and mixing speeds makes all modules run at the slowest one.

ECC (Error-Correcting Code) memory adds an extra chip that can detect and correct single-bit errors (and detect multi-bit errors) on the fly, preventing silent data corruption. It is used in servers and mission-critical systems — appropriate for embassy data-center workloads — and requires CPU and motherboard support. Non-ECC (standard desktop) RAM has no such protection. Parity is an older detect-only scheme. ECC trades a small cost/performance overhead for reliability.

Memory channels multiply bandwidth by accessing multiple modules in parallel. Single-channel uses one stick; dual-channel uses two matched modules in the correctly colored slots to roughly double memory bandwidth; quad-channel (HEDT/server platforms) uses four. The practical rule: install RAM in matched pairs/kits in the slots the manual specifies (often A2/B2) to enable dual-channel — a single stick or mismatched modules can silently halve memory bandwidth and hurt iGPU and overall performance.

Storage is non-volatile (persists without power). A hard disk drive (HDD) stores data magnetically on spinning platters read by an actuator arm with heads. Performance is tied to rotational speed: 5400 RPM (quiet, low-power laptops/archival), 7200 RPM (desktop standard), and 10,000-15,000 RPM (enterprise). HDDs offer the lowest cost-per-terabyte and high capacity, but are slow, fragile (moving parts), and have high seek latency. They remain useful for bulk/archival storage.

A solid-state drive (SSD) uses NAND flash with no moving parts — far faster, silent, shock-resistant, and lower-power, at higher cost-per-TB. The key distinction is the interface, not the chips. A SATA SSD (2.5-inch or mSATA) uses the SATA III bus and is capped at ~600 MB/s (6 Gb/s). An NVMe SSD (Non-Volatile Memory Express) communicates over PCIe lanes, removing the SATA bottleneck and reaching multiple GB/s — several times faster, with far lower latency and deeper command queues. M.2 is a slot/form factor (not an interface): an M.2 SSD can be either SATA or NVMe, distinguished by its key/notch (B-key, M-key, or B+M) — you cannot assume an M.2 drive is NVMe.

Storage form factors and other media to know: 2.5-inch (SATA SSDs and laptop HDDs), 3.5-inch (desktop HDDs), M.2 (gumstick boards, sized like 2280 = 22 mm x 80 mm), and mSATA (older mini SATA). Optical drives (CD ~700 MB, DVD ~4.7 GB single-layer, Blu-ray ~25 GB/layer) are largely legacy. Flash media — USB thumb drives and SD/microSD cards — use the same NAND technology for portable storage; in secure environments these removable devices are often restricted or encrypted because they are easy to lose or use for exfiltration.

RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks) combines drives for performance and/or fault tolerance. RAID 0 (striping) splits data across 2+ disks for maximum speed and full capacity but ZERO redundancy — one disk failure loses everything. RAID 1 (mirroring) duplicates data across 2 disks: full fault tolerance but only 50% usable capacity. RAID 5 (striping with distributed parity, min 3 disks) survives one drive failure and gives usable capacity of (n-1) disks — a common balance of capacity, speed, and protection. RAID 6 (double parity, min 4 disks) survives TWO simultaneous failures, usable capacity (n-2). RAID 10 (1+0, min 4 disks) mirrors then stripes for both speed and redundancy (survives one disk per mirror), with 50% usable capacity — favored for high-I/O databases. RAID is for availability/uptime, NOT a backup; it does not protect against deletion, ransomware, or site disaster.

Before use, a drive must be partitioned with a partition scheme. MBR (Master Boot Record) is the legacy scheme: supports drives up to 2 TB and a maximum of 4 primary partitions (or 3 primary + 1 extended holding logical drives); it pairs with BIOS. GPT (GUID Partition Table) is modern: supports drives far larger than 2 TB and up to 128 partitions, stores redundant partition tables for resilience, and pairs with UEFI (required for UEFI/Secure Boot booting of large drives). Choose GPT for any modern or large-capacity system.

A file system organizes data within a partition. Know the common ones and where they fit: NTFS (Windows default — journaling, permissions/ACLs, encryption (EFS), large file support); FAT32 (universal compatibility but 4 GB max file size and ~2 TB volume limit — used for some boot/USB media); exFAT (FAT successor without the 4 GB limit — ideal for large-file USB/SD media shared across Windows/macOS); ext4 (common Linux file system); and APFS/HFS+ (macOS). Formatting writes a chosen file system; a quick format only clears the index, while a full format also scans for bad sectors and is more secure. The exam pairing to remember is BIOS+MBR+legacy vs UEFI+GPT+Secure Boot for modern systems.
keyPoints: DDR3 (240-pin, 1.5V) / DDR4 (288-pin, 1.2V) / DDR5 (288-pin diff notch, 1.1V); generations are keyed and NOT interchangeable
DIMM = desktop full-size; SO-DIMM = laptop/small (DDR4 SO-DIMM 260-pin, DDR3 SO-DIMM 204-pin)
ECC detects+corrects single-bit errors (servers/critical); non-ECC has no protection; needs CPU/board support
Dual-channel (matched pair in correct slots) ~doubles bandwidth vs single-channel; mismatched RAM runs at the slowest speed
HDD speed = RPM (5400 quiet, 7200 desktop, 10k-15k enterprise); cheapest per TB but slow/fragile
SATA III SSD caps ~600 MB/s (6 Gb/s); NVMe runs over PCIe at multiple GB/s with lower latency
M.2 is a form factor, NOT an interface — an M.2 SSD may be SATA or NVMe (check the key/notch)
RAID 0 = stripe (speed, no redundancy); RAID 1 = mirror (50% usable); RAID 5 = parity, min 3, survives 1; RAID 6 = double parity, min 4, survives 2; RAID 10 = min 4, mirror+stripe
RAID is for uptime/availability, NOT a backup (won't stop deletion/ransomware/disaster)
MBR = 2TB max, 4 primary partitions, BIOS; GPT = >2TB, 128 partitions, redundant tables, UEFI
File systems: NTFS (Windows, ACLs), FAT32 (4GB file limit), exFAT (large-file USB/SD cross-OS), ext4 (Linux), APFS (macOS)
Modern pairing: UEFI + GPT + Secure Boot; legacy pairing: BIOS + MBR

flashcards.front: Pin counts and voltages of DDR3/DDR4/DDR5 DIMMs
flashcards.back: DDR3 = 240-pin @1.5V; DDR4 = 288-pin @1.2V; DDR5 = 288-pin (different notch) @1.1V
DIMM = full-size desktop/server module; SO-DIMM = shorter laptop/mini-PC module (not slot-compatible)
Detects and corrects single-bit errors (detects multi-bit) to prevent silent data corruption; used in servers/critical systems
Roughly doubles memory bandwidth vs single-channel by accessing two matched modules in parallel

flashcards.front: HDD performance is tied to what spec?
flashcards.back: Rotational speed (RPM): 5400 (quiet/laptop), 7200 (desktop), 10k-15k (enterprise)
~600 MB/s (6 Gb/s) — the ceiling for any SATA SSD

flashcards.front: Why is NVMe faster than SATA SSD?
flashcards.back: NVMe runs over PCIe lanes (multiple GB/s) instead of the ~600 MB/s SATA bus, with lower latency and deeper queues

flashcards.front: Is every M.2 SSD an NVMe drive?
flashcards.back: No — M.2 is a form factor; an M.2 SSD can be SATA or NVMe, determined by its key/notch
Striping across 2+ disks: maximum speed and full capacity, but zero redundancy (one failure = total loss)
Mirroring: full fault tolerance, but only 50% usable capacity

flashcards.front: RAID 5: min disks, fault tolerance, capacity
flashcards.back: Min 3 disks; survives one failure; usable capacity = (n-1) disks via distributed parity
RAID 6 uses double parity (min 4 disks) and survives TWO simultaneous failures; usable capacity (n-2)
Mirror + stripe (min 4 disks): high speed and redundancy, 50% usable; favored for high-I/O databases
No — RAID provides availability/uptime only; it does not protect against deletion, ransomware, or site disaster
Drives up to 2 TB and a max of 4 primary partitions (or 3 primary + 1 extended); pairs with BIOS
Supports drives >2 TB, up to 128 partitions, redundant partition tables; required for UEFI/Secure Boot booting of large drives
FAT32 has a 4 GB max file size; exFAT removes that limit and is ideal for large-file USB/SD media across Windows/macOS

flashcards.front: Default Windows file system and its features
flashcards.back: NTFS — journaling, permissions/ACLs, encryption (EFS), and large file support
Quick clears the index only; full also scans for bad sectors and overwrites more thoroughly (more secure, slower)

title: Power, Cooling, Cables & Connectors (PSU, 80 PLUS, UPS, USB/Thunderbolt, Video, Networking)
summary: Covers PSU wattage/efficiency/connectors, redundant power and UPS, cooling methods, and the full range of USB/Thunderbolt, video, data, and network cables/ports plus expansion cards.
content:
The power supply unit (PSU) converts wall AC (120/230 V) into the regulated low-voltage DC the system uses: +3.3 V, +5 V, and +12 V (the +12 V rail carries the heaviest load — CPU and GPU). Wattage must exceed the system's combined draw with headroom (a basic office desktop may need 300-450 W; a workstation with a discrete GPU 650 W+). Undersized PSUs cause random reboots, shutdowns under load, or no-boot. A modular PSU lets you attach only the cables you need for cleaner airflow; the form factor (ATX PSU) must fit the case.

Efficiency is certified by 80 PLUS, meaning the PSU is at least 80% efficient (the rest is wasted as heat) across its load range. Tiers improve from 80 PLUS (White), Bronze, Silver, Gold, Platinum, to Titanium — higher tiers waste less energy and run cooler/quieter, which matters for a room full of always-on embassy systems (lower power bills and heat output). A 'rail' is a power output line; multi-rail designs split +12 V into separately current-limited rails for safety, while single-rail provides one large +12 V output.

Know the power connectors by sight and purpose: the 24-pin ATX connector is the main motherboard power (older boards used 20-pin; 20+4 adapters exist). The 4-pin or 8-pin EPS (CPU/ATX12V) connector powers the CPU near the socket. PCIe power connectors (6-pin, 8-pin/6+2, and the newer 12VHPWR/12V-2x6 on high-end GPUs) feed graphics cards that need more than the slot's 75 W. SATA power connectors (flat, 15-pin) power drives; the older 4-pin Molex powers legacy drives, fans, and some accessories. Matching the right connector to each component is essential — never force a connector.

For availability, two technologies appear on the exam. Redundant PSUs (common in servers, hot-swappable, often labeled 1+1) let a second supply take over instantly if one fails, so a critical server stays up — valuable for an embassy's always-on infrastructure. A UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) is a battery backup that provides instant power during an outage and protects against sags/surges/brownouts, giving time to ride out short blips or to shut down gracefully (often signaling the OS to auto-shutdown). A UPS is for short runtime and clean shutdown, not long-term power — that role belongs to a generator. A surge protector only clamps voltage spikes and provides no battery backup.

Heat is the enemy of electronics; cooling keeps components within safe thermal limits. Air cooling is most common: a heatsink (finned metal) draws heat away and a fan moves air across it (the CPU heatsink-and-fan, plus case fans). Liquid cooling (AIO closed-loop or custom loop) uses a pump to circulate coolant through a CPU/GPU block to a radiator — more capacity for high-TDP chips. Thermal paste between the chip's heat spreader and the cooler is mandatory for good heat transfer. Good airflow follows a front-to-back, bottom-to-top path (cool intake in front/bottom, warm exhaust at rear/top); positive case pressure (more intake than exhaust) reduces dust. Blocked vents, dust buildup, dead fans, or dried paste cause overheating, throttling, and shutdowns.

USB (Universal Serial Bus) is the universal peripheral interface; learn the speed tiers (the naming is notoriously confusing): USB 2.0 = 480 Mbps (Hi-Speed); USB 3.0/3.1 Gen 1 (USB 3.2 Gen 1) = 5 Gbps (SuperSpeed, blue ports); USB 3.1 Gen 2 (USB 3.2 Gen 2) = 10 Gbps; USB 3.2 Gen 2x2 = 20 Gbps; USB4 = up to 40 Gbps. USB-C is a reversible connector (a physical shape, not a speed) that can carry USB data, DisplayPort video (Alt Mode), and up to 100 W of USB Power Delivery on one cable. Thunderbolt 3/4 uses the USB-C connector and delivers 40 Gbps with PCIe and DisplayPort tunneling for high-end docks and external GPUs/storage — a USB-C port is not necessarily Thunderbolt (look for the lightning-bolt icon).

Video connectors: HDMI carries digital video and audio together (consumer/TV standard, supports HDCP copy protection); DisplayPort (DP) is the PC/monitor digital standard, supports higher refresh rates and daisy-chaining (MST), and Mini-DP/USB-C Alt Mode variants exist. VGA is the legacy 15-pin analog (blue) connector — degrades over distance, found on projectors and older KVMs. DVI bridges the eras (DVI-D digital, DVI-A analog, DVI-I both). For diplomatic AV setups, DisplayPort/HDMI are preferred for digital quality, with adapters bridging legacy VGA projectors.

Data and network cabling: SATA data cables are flat 7-pin cables (with an L-shaped key) connecting drives to the board, separate from SATA power. Network uses RJ45 connectors on twisted-pair Ethernet rated by category: Cat5e (1 Gbps to 100 m), Cat6 (1 Gbps to 100 m, 10 Gbps to ~55 m), Cat6a (10 Gbps to 100 m); RJ11 is the smaller telephone connector. Legacy/serial interfaces still appear on network and embassy gear: DB9 serial (RS-232) for console access to routers/switches/PBX, and the old DB25 parallel/serial. Recognizing these — and that console management often still rides RS-232 — is exam-relevant.

Expansion cards add capability via PCIe slots when integrated options are insufficient. A GPU (graphics/video card) goes in the x16 slot for 3D, multi-monitor, or GPU compute and usually needs supplemental PCIe power. A NIC (network interface card) adds wired/fiber/extra Ethernet or higher-speed networking. A sound card adds audio I/O beyond onboard audio. A capture card ingests external video (HDMI/SDI) for recording/streaming — useful for recording diplomatic briefings or video conferencing feeds. Match the card's PCIe lane requirement to an available slot, seat it fully, secure the bracket, and install drivers.
keyPoints: PSU converts AC to DC rails +3.3V/+5V/+12V (+12V carries CPU/GPU); undersized PSU = reboots/shutdowns under load
80 PLUS tiers (White>Bronze>Silver>Gold>Platinum>Titanium) certify >=80% efficiency; higher = less heat/power waste
Connectors: 24-pin ATX (board), 4/8-pin EPS (CPU), 6/8-pin PCIe & 12VHPWR (GPU), 15-pin SATA power (drives), 4-pin Molex (legacy)
Redundant PSUs (1+1, hot-swap) keep servers running; a UPS = battery backup for clean shutdown/ride-through, not long runtime
Surge protector only clamps spikes (no battery); generator = long-term power
Cooling: heatsink+fan (air), AIO/liquid for high TDP; thermal paste mandatory; airflow front/bottom in, rear/top out
USB speeds: 2.0=480Mbps, 3.x Gen1=5Gbps, Gen2=10Gbps, 3.2 2x2=20Gbps, USB4=40Gbps
USB-C = reversible connector (can do data + DisplayPort Alt Mode + 100W PD); Thunderbolt 3/4 = 40Gbps over USB-C (not all USB-C is TB)
Video: HDMI (digital A/V, HDCP), DisplayPort (PC standard, daisy-chain MST), VGA (legacy analog), DVI (D/A/I bridge)
Ethernet RJ45 by category: Cat5e/Cat6=1Gbps (Cat6 10G to ~55m), Cat6a=10Gbps/100m; RJ11=phone
Legacy DB9 serial (RS-232) still used for console access to routers/switches/PBX
Expansion cards via PCIe: GPU (x16), NIC, sound card, capture card; seat fully, secure bracket, install drivers

flashcards.front: Which PSU rail carries the heaviest load?
flashcards.back: The +12 V rail (powers CPU and GPU); PSUs also provide +3.3 V and +5 V
The PSU is at least 80% efficient; tiers rise White > Bronze > Silver > Gold > Platinum > Titanium (less waste heat)
Random reboots, shutdowns under load, or failure to boot
The 4-pin or 8-pin EPS/ATX12V connector near the socket (main board power is the 24-pin ATX)
15-pin flat SATA power for modern drives; 4-pin Molex for legacy drives/fans/accessories
Redundant PSU (1+1, hot-swap) keeps a server running if one supply fails; a UPS is a battery for ride-through and graceful shutdown

flashcards.front: UPS vs surge protector vs generator
flashcards.back: UPS = battery backup + surge protection (short runtime); surge protector = spike clamping only; generator = long-term power
It transfers heat from the chip's heat spreader to the cooler by filling air gaps; without it the CPU overheats and throttles
Cool intake at front/bottom, warm exhaust at rear/top; positive pressure (more intake) reduces dust
USB 2.0 = 480 Mbps; USB 3.0/3.2 Gen 1 = 5 Gbps (SuperSpeed, blue ports)
USB 3.2 Gen 2 = 10 Gbps; Gen 2x2 = 20 Gbps; USB4 = up to 40 Gbps

flashcards.front: Is USB-C a speed or a connector?
flashcards.back: A reversible connector shape; it can carry data, DisplayPort Alt Mode video, and up to 100 W Power Delivery
40 Gbps interface over the USB-C connector tunneling PCIe + DisplayPort; not every USB-C port is Thunderbolt (look for the bolt icon)
HDMI = digital video+audio, consumer/TV, HDCP; DisplayPort = PC standard, higher refresh and daisy-chaining (MST)
Legacy 15-pin analog video (blue connector); degrades over distance, found on projectors/older KVMs
DVI-D = digital only; DVI-A = analog only; DVI-I = integrated (both)
Cat5e = 1 Gbps/100m; Cat6 = 1 Gbps/100m (10G to ~55m); Cat6a = 10 Gbps/100m (RJ45 connector)

flashcards.front: What is DB9 / RS-232 used for?
flashcards.back: Legacy serial console access to routers, switches, and PBX equipment
GPU (x16), NIC (networking), sound card (audio), capture card (external video ingest)

title: Printers & Peripherals (Laser 7-Step Imaging, Inkjet/Thermal/Impact, 3D, Spooler, Troubleshooting)
summary: Covers laser printing's seven-step imaging process, inkjet/thermal/impact and 3D printing, consumables and maintenance, connectivity/sharing/drivers/spooler, scanners and KVM, and printer troubleshooting.
content:
Printers convert digital data to physical output via different technologies, and the laser printer's imaging process is the single most-tested item. A laser printer uses an electrophotographic process with a photosensitive imaging drum, a laser, charged toner (fine plastic powder), and heat. Output quality is measured in DPI (dots per inch) and speed in PPM (pages per minute). Knowing the consumables and the seven-step cycle in order is essential.

The seven steps of laser imaging, in exact order (mnemonic: 'Processing, Charging, Exposing, Developing, Transferring, Fusing, Cleaning'): (1) Processing — the printer's raster image processor builds the full-page bitmap in memory before printing. (2) Charging — the primary charge roller (or corona wire) applies a uniform high negative charge across the drum. (3) Exposing (writing) — the laser scans the drum, discharging the points that will hold toner, forming a latent electrostatic image. (4) Developing — the developer roller coats the discharged areas with charged toner. (5) Transferring — a charge (transfer roller/belt) pulls the toner from the drum onto the paper. (6) Fusing — the fuser unit's heat (~180-200 C) and pressure rollers melt the toner permanently into the paper (which is why pages exit warm). (7) Cleaning — a blade and discharge lamp remove residual toner and neutralize the drum for the next page.

Other print technologies: an inkjet printer sprays microscopic droplets of liquid ink from a print head (thermal-bubble or piezoelectric) onto paper — cheap hardware, excellent color/photo quality, but slow and with costly ink that can dry/clog; maintenance includes head cleaning and nozzle-check alignment. A thermal printer uses heat: direct thermal heats special heat-sensitive paper (receipts, shipping/visa labels — fades over time, no ink) while thermal transfer melts a ribbon onto media for durable labels. An impact/dot-matrix printer strikes an inked ribbon against paper with pins — noisy and low quality, but the only type that prints multi-part carbon forms, so it survives in customs/visa offices and for continuous-feed forms.

3D printing builds objects layer by layer (additive manufacturing). FDM/FFF (Fused Deposition Modeling) extrudes melted thermoplastic filament (PLA, ABS, PETG) through a heated nozzle onto a build plate — affordable and common. Resin printing (SLA/DLP) cures liquid photopolymer resin with UV light layer by layer for high detail, but requires post-processing (washing and UV curing) and handling of toxic uncured resin. Both build on a heated/leveled bed and need slicer software to convert a 3D model into machine instructions (G-code).

Consumables and maintenance: laser printers use toner cartridges (often integrated with the drum) and periodically a maintenance kit (fuser, transfer/feed rollers, pickup rollers) installed at a rated page count to prevent jams and quality loss; a counter is then reset. Inkjets use ink cartridges and benefit from head cleaning. Calibration aligns color and print head position for accurate output. Routine maintenance — replacing worn rollers, clearing dust/debris, and running the manufacturer's cleaning/calibration cycle — keeps a busy embassy print room reliable.

Connectivity and sharing: printers connect via USB (direct to one PC), wired Ethernet or Wi-Fi (networked, shared by many), or Bluetooth. A networked printer has its own IP address and is the norm in offices. A print server (a dedicated box, a server role, or a PC sharing a printer) centralizes queuing and driver distribution for many users — useful for controlling and auditing printing of sensitive documents. Each client needs the correct printer driver (the software translating print jobs into the printer's page-description language such as PCL or PostScript); a wrong/corrupt driver produces garbled output. Secure/follow-me printing (release a job with a badge/PIN at the device) is common where confidentiality matters.

The print spooler is the OS service (spoolsv.exe on Windows) that queues print jobs and feeds them to the printer in order, letting you keep working while printing. A stuck spooler is a classic fault: jobs pile up and nothing prints. The fix is to stop the Print Spooler service, delete the stuck files in the spool folder (C:\Windows\System32\spool\PRINTERS), then restart the service. Spooler/queue issues — not the hardware — are a frequent cause of 'printer not responding.'

Other peripherals to know: a scanner digitizes documents/photos (flatbed, sheet-fed/ADF for batches, or an all-in-one MFP combining print/scan/copy/fax); resolution is in DPI and TWAIN/WIA are the common driver standards. A KVM (Keyboard, Video, Mouse) switch lets one keyboard/monitor/mouse control multiple computers — valuable in a secure setting to operate separate classified and unclassified machines from one desk (a 'secure KVM' enforces isolation between them). Other common peripherals include webcams, headsets, signature pads, smart-card/CAC readers (for diplomatic credentials), barcode scanners, and biometric readers.

Printer troubleshooting maps symptom to cause to fix. Vertical streaks or lines on laser output usually mean a scratched/dirty drum or contaminated fuser — replace the toner/drum or clean the path. Ghosting (a faint repeated image down the page) points to a failing drum or worn fuser that isn't fully erasing/fusing — replace the imaging unit or maintenance kit. Toner that smears or rubs off was not fused — a failed/cold fuser (replace it). Repeated paper jams come from worn pickup/feed rollers, wrong paper weight, humidity, or debris in the path — replace rollers and use proper, dry media. Faded/light print means low toner (or economode) — replace toner or disable toner-save. Garbled or gibberish output is a driver/language mismatch or corrupt job — install the correct driver and clear the spooler. Blank pages can mean no toner or the sealing tape left on a new cartridge. Always print a test/self-test page to isolate whether the fault is the printer, the driver, the connection, or the application.
keyPoints: Laser 7 steps in order: Processing, Charging, Exposing, Developing, Transferring, Fusing, Cleaning
Charging = uniform negative charge; Exposing = laser writes latent image; Fusing = heat (~180-200C) + pressure melts toner in
Inkjet sprays liquid ink (great photos, ink clogs); thermal uses heat (direct=receipts/labels, fade; transfer=durable ribbon)
Impact/dot-matrix strikes pins through a ribbon — only type that prints multi-part carbon forms (visa/customs)
3D: FDM extrudes melted filament (PLA/ABS); resin (SLA/DLP) UV-cures liquid resin (high detail, toxic, needs post-cure)
Laser maintenance kit (fuser + rollers) replaced at a page count, then reset the counter; inkjets need head cleaning
Connectivity: USB (one PC), Ethernet/Wi-Fi (networked, has its own IP), print server centralizes queues/drivers
Driver translates jobs to PCL/PostScript; wrong/corrupt driver = garbled output
Print spooler (spoolsv.exe) queues jobs; stuck spooler fix = stop service, clear spool\PRINTERS, restart service
Scanners (flatbed/ADF/MFP) use DPI and TWAIN/WIA; KVM shares one keyboard/monitor/mouse across PCs (secure KVM isolates classified/unclassified)
Streaks = dirty/scratched drum or fuser; ghosting = failing drum/worn fuser; smearing = bad/cold fuser
Paper jams = worn pickup/feed rollers, wrong/humid paper; faded print = low toner/economode; print a self-test page to isolate

flashcards.front: The seven laser imaging steps in order
flashcards.back: Processing, Charging, Exposing, Developing, Transferring, Fusing, Cleaning

flashcards.front: What happens in the Charging step?
flashcards.back: The primary charge roller/corona wire applies a uniform negative charge across the imaging drum

flashcards.front: What happens in the Exposing (writing) step?
flashcards.back: The laser discharges points on the drum to form the latent electrostatic image
Uses heat (~180-200 C) and pressure to permanently melt toner into the paper (why pages exit warm)
A blade and discharge lamp remove residual toner and neutralize the drum's charge for the next page

flashcards.front: How does an inkjet printer work, and its main weakness?
flashcards.back: Sprays liquid ink droplets (thermal/piezo head); great color/photos but slow and ink can dry/clog the head
Direct thermal heats special paper (receipts/labels, fades); thermal transfer melts a ribbon onto media for durable output
They strike pins through a ribbon, so they can print multi-part carbon forms (visa/customs/continuous feed)
FDM extrudes melted thermoplastic filament; resin UV-cures liquid photopolymer for high detail but needs washing/curing and is toxic

flashcards.front: What is a printer maintenance kit?
flashcards.back: A set of wear parts (fuser, transfer/feed/pickup rollers) replaced at a rated page count to prevent jams/quality loss; reset the counter after

flashcards.front: What does a print server provide?
flashcards.back: Centralized queuing and driver distribution for many users; aids control/auditing of sensitive printing
Wrong or corrupt driver / page-description language mismatch (PCL vs PostScript), or a corrupt spooled job
The OS service (spoolsv.exe) that queues print jobs and feeds them to the printer in order

flashcards.front: How to fix a stuck print spooler
flashcards.back: Stop the Print Spooler service, delete files in C:\Windows\System32\spool\PRINTERS, then restart the service
Keyboard/Video/Mouse switch — controls multiple PCs from one set of peripherals; a secure KVM isolates classified vs unclassified systems

flashcards.front: Scanner driver standards and resolution unit
flashcards.back: TWAIN and WIA drivers; resolution measured in DPI (dots per inch)

flashcards.front: Cause of vertical streaks/lines on laser prints
flashcards.back: A scratched or dirty imaging drum or a contaminated fuser/path — replace toner/drum or clean
A failing drum or worn fuser not fully erasing/fusing — replace the imaging unit or maintenance kit

flashcards.front: Toner smears/rubs off the page — cause?
flashcards.back: The toner was not fused — a failed or cold fuser; replace the fuser unit

flashcards.front: Common causes of repeated paper jams
flashcards.back: Worn pickup/feed rollers, wrong paper weight, humidity, or debris in the paper path

flashcards.front: First step to isolate a printing problem
flashcards.back: Print a self-test/test page to determine if the fault is the printer, driver, connection, or application
